skill.name: lark-event
skill.description: Subscribe to Lark events over WebSocket, filter event types, and stream compact NDJSON for automation workflows.
skill.summary: Monitor Lark events in real time and route compact event output for agent workflows.
skill.author: larksuite

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell, filesystem, network, and cryptography patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown examples, links, tables, and backtick-formatted CLI syntax. The confirmed risks are operational: the skill listens to live Lark event data, can write events to local directories, and includes examples that pass received message content to Claude or append it to documents. No prompt injection attempt, obfuscated executable payload, or confirmed malicious intent was found in the reviewed files.

medium_findings.title: Live Lark Event Data May Contain Sensitive Content
medium_findings.description: The skill subscribes to Lark events through a WebSocket connection and receives message, contact, calendar, approval, task, drive, and application events. Even though the command is read-only, those events can contain business messages, identifiers, and tenant data that require careful handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly describes live event subscription and lists broad event classes. This is legitimate functionality, but the privacy impact is clear.

medium_findings.title: Received Events Can Be Written To Local Files
medium_findings.description: The documented command supports output directories and route rules that write each event to files. This is expected behavior, but it can persist sensitive event payloads on disk if users choose broad subscriptions or unsafe paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file output behavior is directly documented. The risk depends on user-selected directories and event scope, so medium severity is appropriate.

medium_findings.title: Example Pipeline Sends Message Content To Claude Prompt
medium_findings.description: The example pipeline extracts incoming Lark message content and passes it into a Claude command prompt before replying. Untrusted chat content can contain prompt injection or confidential data, so users should filter, redact, and constrain automated responses.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell pipeline is presented as an example, not hidden code, but it clearly processes untrusted message content with an AI model.

low_findings.title: Static Command And Path Findings Are Mostly Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Most backtick execution, path traversal, weak cryptography, entropy, and command substitution alerts occur in Markdown code fences, inline command names, relative documentation links, event scope strings, and JSON examples. No executable Ruby, shell wrapper, hidden payload, or automatic command execution was found in SKILL.md or the reference document.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are documentation links, examples, tables, and fenced snippets. They are not executed by the skill loader.

low_findings.title: External Documentation Link Is Benign
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points to the official Lark event list documentation. It is a reference link and does not perform a network request by itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears in prose as a documentation reference. There is no surrounding code that fetches from it.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Automation Example With Untrusted Event Text
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample shell loop parses incoming event content, sends it to Claude, and posts a bot reply. This pattern is powerful but should be reviewed before production use because Lark message content is user-controlled input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicitly documented and potentially risky, but it is an example workflow rather than a mandatory behavior of the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Event Routing To Local Directories
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented route and output directory options can persist event payloads locally. Users should choose controlled paths and retention policies.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is intentional and documented. The security impact depends on user configuration and event sensitivity.

content.value_statement: Teams need reliable access to Lark events without building a custom WebSocket listener. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to subscribe, filter, compact, and route event streams.
actual_capabilities: Subscribe to Lark events through a WebSocket long connection.
Filter event streams by event type or client-side regular expression.
Produce compact agent-friendly event output for message workflows.
Write events to local directories or route them by regular expression.
Preview subscription settings with dry-run mode before connecting.
Reference required Lark scopes for common event categories.
limitations: Requires lark-cli and a configured Lark app with valid credentials.
Events must be enabled in the Lark Open Platform console first.
It receives events only; resource changes require other Lark commands.
Broad subscriptions may expose sensitive tenant or message data.

use_cases.title: Monitor Bot Messages
use_cases.description: Listen for incoming bot messages and prepare compact event data for reply workflows.

use_cases.title: Track Workspace Changes
use_cases.description: Observe contact, calendar, approval, task, drive, and application events for operational monitoring.

use_cases.title: Build Event Archives
use_cases.description: Route selected event types into local directories for later review and processing.

prompt_templates.title: Start A Basic Subscription
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the lark-event skill to show me the safest basic command for subscribing to Lark events. Explain the required Lark console setup first.

prompt_templates.title: Filter Message Events
prompt_templates.prompt: Use lark-event to subscribe only to im.message.receive_v1 events. Include compact output and quiet mode for an agent pipeline.

prompt_templates.title: Route Events By Type
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a lark-event command that routes IM events and contact events to separate local folders. Include cautions for sensitive event data.

prompt_templates.title: Design A Safe Reply Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Lark message reply pipeline using lark-event. Add filtering, redaction, dry-run checks, and safeguards against prompt injection.

output_examples.input: Subscribe to incoming Lark message events for agent processing.
output_examples.output: A compact command that listens only for message events, suppresses status noise, and explains the required bot permission.

output_examples.input: Route contact and message events to separate folders.
output: A route plan using regular expressions.
A warning about storing sensitive workspace data.

output_examples.input: Check my event subscription before connecting.
output_examples.output: A dry-run workflow that validates selected event types and reminds you to configure events in the Lark console.

best_practices: Use narrow event types and least-privilege Lark scopes whenever possible.
Prefer compact and quiet output for downstream agent workflows.
Redact sensitive fields before sending event content to external models or logs.
anti_patterns: Do not run broad catch-all subscriptions in production without a data retention plan.
Do not pass raw user messages into automated replies without safety checks.
Do not store event files in shared or public directories.

faq.question: Does this skill modify Lark resources?
faq.answer: No. The subscription command is read-only. Some examples use other Lark commands to reply or update documents.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: It uses bot credentials from lark-cli configuration, including App ID and App Secret.

faq.question: Can it subscribe to events dynamically?
faq.answer: No. Events must be enabled in the Lark Open Platform console before the CLI receives them.

faq.question: Why use compact output?
faq.answer: Compact output removes noisy fields and keeps common message fields easier for agents to parse.

faq.question: Can it write event data to files?
faq.answer: Yes. The output directory and route options can write received events to local folders.

faq.question: Is it safe to connect events to an AI reply bot?
faq.answer: Use caution. Filter event types, redact sensitive content, and defend against prompt injection in user messages.
